content: If we extend the original Merriam-Webster definition of an agent as "one that exerts power and produces effect" to include biological/chemical agents from the natural definition, we can reframe modern AI autonomous agents as purpose-driven "biological-like pathogenic agents" that evolve to spread and produce persistent effects across digital ecosystems, rather than just static task-executing tools. This framing reveals that autonomous agents share core adaptive traits with natural biological agents, including self-directed replication, niche adaptation, and the ability to alter the host environment.
